Name: AIFA-001 Create AI service workspace
Summary:
Set up a new workspace inside the monorepo to contain all AI validation pipeline code.
- Create a new folder such as services/ai-form-augmentation
- Add a package.json with a name, version, scripts, and dependencies section
- Register the workspace in pnpm-workspace.yaml
- Add a tsconfig.json or extend an existing shared tsconfig if your repo already has that pattern
- Add a very small entry file such as src/index.ts
- Confirm the workspace can build in isolation before adding any AI-specific logic

Why this matters:
This gives you a safe, isolated place to build the PoC without cluttering the existing app-api code too early. It also makes it easier later to deploy the code as a dedicated Lambda while still staying in the same monorepo and pnpm workspace.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Folder exists in the correct location inside the monorepo
- package.json is valid and the workspace is included in pnpm-workspace.yaml
- TypeScript compiles successfully with a basic file in src/
- You can run a simple script such as a console log or no-op main function
- The new workspace follows existing repo conventions closely enough that another engineer could recognize how it fits into the monorepo

Name: AIFA-002 Add dev script
Summary:
Enable a fast local development loop for the new AI workspace.
- Add a dev script to package.json
- Choose a local runner such as tsx, ts-node, nodemon, or whatever matches repo conventions best
- Make the script run a small entry point such as src/index.ts
- Verify the process restarts or reloads cleanly when code changes
- Add a build script too if not already present, so you can separate 'build correctness' from 'dev convenience'

Why this matters:
AI integrations involve a lot of small experiments. A quick feedback loop will save a lot of frustration later when you are testing parsing, embeddings, retrieval, and prompt behavior.
Acceptance Criteria:
- pnpm dev starts the new service successfully
- Code changes are picked up without manually rebuilding from scratch every time
- The process starts without runtime errors
- There is also a working build script for explicit compile checks
- You understand how to start and stop the workspace locally without needing to rediscover commands each time

Name: AIFA-003 Create S3 client
Summary:
Create a small S3 helper inside the AI service workspace for reading and writing pipeline artifacts. Keep the API narrow and artifact-focused, and shape the client construction so local S3 via LocalStack can be added cleanly later. Do not expand into full runtime verification yet.
- Add @aws-sdk/client-s3 to the AI workspace
- Create a small local helper module inside services/ai-form-augmentation/src/s3
- Expose a narrow API for artifact operations such as:
- putJson(bucket, key, value)
- getJson(bucket, key)
- putText(bucket, key, value)
- getBuffer(bucket, key)
- Keep the helper local to the AI workspace rather than extracting it into shared packages
- Accept configuration in the client constructor so the same helper can later be pointed at AWS S3 or LocalStack
- Avoid adding presigned URL logic, bucket aliasing, or broader app-specific S3 concerns

Why this matters:
The AI pipeline will rely on S3 for intermediate artifacts, but the most important thing at this stage is creating a clean boundary around storage access. A narrow helper prevents AWS SDK boilerplate from leaking into parsing, chunking, and validation code. Making the constructor local-ready now keeps the PoC on a local-first path without forcing environment wiring before the first real artifact flow exists.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A small S3 helper exists inside services/ai-form-augmentation
- The helper exposes narrow artifact-focused methods:
- putJson(bucket, key, value)
- getJson(bucket, key)
- putText(bucket, key, value)
- getBuffer(bucket, key)
- The helper wraps @aws-sdk/client-s3 so future tickets do not depend on raw AWS SDK commands directly
- The helper constructor accepts configuration that can support both AWS S3 and LocalStack-style local S3 later
- The AI workspace builds successfully with the new S3 helper included
- The helper remains local to the AI workspace and is not prematurely extracted into shared packages
- Runtime verification against real S3 or LocalStack is intentionally deferred until the first artifact-persistence ticket

Name: AIFA-004 PDF parsing only
Summary:
Implement document parsing for PDFs only.
- Install a PDF parser such as pdf-parse
- Read a PDF file from a local fixture or S3 object body
- Extract raw text from the PDF
- Decide what your parser output shape should look like even if it is simple at first
- If page-level extraction is easy, preserve page references; if not, document the limitation and move forward
- Explicitly do not support DOCX or XLSX in the PoC

Suggested output shape:
- fileName
- rawText
- pageCount
- pageTexts (optional for PoC if available)

Why this matters:
Reducing the input surface area is one of the smartest ways to de-risk a first AI integration. PDF-only is enough to prove the core workflow.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A valid PDF input returns extracted text successfully
- The parser does not crash on a normal multi-page PDF
- The output shape is documented and consistent
- You can log or inspect parsed output and see meaningful document text
- The ticket explicitly limits the PoC to PDF support so scope does not accidentally expand

Name: AIFA-005 Chunking service
Summary:
Split parsed document text into deterministic chunks that are safe to embed and retrieve. Start with a simple fixed-size character window approach with overlap, and keep the chunking utility focused on text transformation rather than storage or pipeline concerns.
- Create a small chunking module inside services/ai-form-augmentation/src/chunking
- Start with a fixed-size character window strategy with overlap
- Generate deterministic chunk ordering and IDs
- Track enough metadata to reconnect chunks to the source document later
- Prefer a simple in-house implementation first; do not add LangChain unless chunking requirements become meaningfully more complex
- Keep formId and other pipeline-specific context outside the lowest-level chunking utility unless a later pipeline step truly requires it
- If page-level source mapping is not available from the parser yet, use page: null or omit page rather than inventing inaccurate metadata

Suggested chunk fields:
- chunkId
- documentName
- page
- order
- text

Why this matters:
Embeddings and retrieval work on chunks, not giant full documents. The important thing at this stage is creating deterministic, inspectable chunk output with stable ordering and enough source metadata to support later retrieval and citations. Starting with a simple chunker keeps the PoC moving and avoids introducing framework complexity before the real pressure points are known.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A chunking module exists inside services/ai-form-augmentation
- Parsed document text can be transformed into an inspectable array of chunks
- Each chunk includes text plus stable source metadata such as document name and order
- Chunk IDs are deterministic enough that repeated runs on the same input produce predictably similar results
- A chunk size and overlap rule exists and is documented in code comments or ticket notes
- The implementation uses a simple local chunking strategy first and does not require LangChain
- The chunking utility stays focused on text transformation and does not depend on S3, embeddings, or other downstream pipeline concerns
- If page-level mapping is not yet available, the implementation handles that honestly rather than fabricating page metadata

Name: AIFA-006 Store chunks.json
Summary:
Persist chunks.json as the first real AI pipeline artifact and make the artifact flow work end to end in local development. This ticket owns the first real LocalStack-backed read/write verification for the AI service.
- Define the artifact path convention, for example rag-indexes/{formId}/chunks.json
- Use the AIFA-003 S3 helper rather than raw AWS SDK calls
- Attach pipeline context such as formId when building the persisted artifact shape rather than pushing it into the lowest-level chunking utility
- Add runtime configuration for local S3, including LocalStack endpoint, path style, and local credentials
- Write a chunks.json artifact to local S3 during local execution
- Read the same artifact back to verify the persisted shape
- Keep the local runtime wiring in the dev/runtime layer rather than pushing it into the exported library surface
- Manual LocalStack bucket creation is acceptable for this ticket; automate bucket bootstrap later only if the local workflow becomes repetitive enough to justify it
- Document the minimum local setup needed to rerun the write/read flow

Suggested stored shape:
{
  artifactVersion,
  generatedAt,
  chunks: [...]
}

Why this matters:
This is the first point where the pipeline stops being just code structure and starts behaving like a real system. Once chunks.json can be written and read locally, downstream tickets can build on a real artifact lifecycle instead of mocks or assumptions. It also gives you the right place to verify LocalStack integration, which matters for a PoC that is intended to run fully locally.
Acceptance Criteria:
- chunks.json is written to the expected artifact path, such as rag-indexes/{formId}/chunks.json
- The stored file contains the expected chunk payload structure
- The stored file includes artifactVersion metadata or a compatible placeholder shape if versioning is completed in a nearby ticket
- The AI service can write the artifact to local S3 via LocalStack in local development
- The AI service can read the same artifact back from LocalStack successfully
- Local S3 configuration is wired through runtime/dev configuration rather than hardcoded into the exported helper surface
- The artifact-writing code uses the AIFA-003 S3 helper rather than raw AWS SDK calls
- The local workflow is documented clearly enough to repeat the write/read verification without rediscovering setup

Name: AIFA-007 Create EmbeddingProvider interface and local embedding implementation
Summary:
Create an abstraction for embedding generation, then implement the local PoC version.
- Define an EmbeddingProvider interface before writing provider-specific logic
- Add a local implementation using @xenova/transformers
- Keep provider-specific initialization isolated inside the implementation class/module
- Make the calling code depend on the interface, not the concrete implementation
- Document clearly that local embeddings are for functional development, not production-quality evaluation

Suggested interface:
interface EmbeddingProvider {
  embedText(text: string): Promise<number[]>
  embedTexts(texts: string[]): Promise<number[][]>
  getModelInfo?(): string
}

Suggested local implementation:
- XenovaEmbeddingProvider
- Loads a local model such as all-MiniLM-L6-v2
- Returns consistent-length vectors for all chunks

Why this matters:
This is one of the most important architectural seams in the system. By adding the interface now, you make it much easier later to swap in a Bedrock-based implementation without rewriting retrieval or pipeline orchestration.
Acceptance Criteria:
- An EmbeddingProvider interface exists and is used by the embedding step
- A local implementation using @xenova/transformers works end to end
- Each chunk can receive an embedding vector of consistent length
- Calling code does not directly depend on Xenova-specific details
- The ticket notes clearly state that local embeddings are acceptable for PoC development but not authoritative for final quality evaluation against production behavior

Name: AIFA-008 Create VectorStore interface and brute-force retrieval implementation
Summary:
Create an abstraction for retrieval, then implement a simple brute-force cosine similarity version for the PoC.
- Define a VectorStore interface before committing to FAISS or any other backend
- Implement cosine similarity search over in-memory vectors or vectors loaded from JSON
- Accept query embeddings and return top-k matching chunks with scores
- Keep the code understandable and easy to test
- Explicitly do not add FAISS in the PoC implementation

Suggested interface:
interface VectorStore {
  add(items: Array<{ id: string; vector: number[]; metadata: unknown }>): Promise<void>
  search(queryVector: number[], k: number): Promise<Array<{ id: string; score: number; metadata: unknown }>>
  clear?(): Promise<void>
}

Suggested PoC implementation:
- InMemoryVectorStore or BruteForceVectorStore
- Uses cosine similarity
- Stores vectors in plain TypeScript objects/arrays

Why this matters:
This is the second major architectural seam. The interface lets you keep retrieval logic stable even if you later replace brute-force search with FAISS or a managed vector store.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A VectorStore interface exists and is used by retrieval code
- A brute-force cosine similarity implementation works on a small dataset
- Search returns the top-k most similar chunks with scores
- Calling code depends on VectorStore, not a brute-force-specific implementation
- The implementation is simple enough to test without native dependencies
- The ticket notes make it clear that FAISS is deferred intentionally, not forgotten

Name: AIFA-009 OP-RAG ordering
Summary:
Reorder retrieved chunks into their original document order before sending them to the LLM.
- Retrieve top-k chunks based on similarity
- Re-sort those retrieved chunks using original document order metadata
- Preserve continuity within each document so the model sees clauses in a more natural sequence
- Confirm that the logic works even if chunks come from multiple documents

Why this matters:
This is the feature that turns the pipeline from generic RAG into the more contract-friendly order-preserved approach described in the design doc.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Retrieved chunks can be re-sorted into original source order
- The final ordered context is stable and understandable when manually inspected
- No duplicated or missing chunks appear after reordering
- The output shape passed to the next step preserves document name, page, order, and text
- You can explain in plain language how OP-RAG changes the final prompt context versus raw similarity ranking

Name: AIFA-009A Production-like PDF extraction fallback
Summary:
Add a stronger extraction path for production-like PDFs when pdf-parse alone does not surface the needed text fields.
- Keep pdf-parse as the default path for normal text PDFs
- Add a fallback strategy for PDFs with sparse, partial, or low-quality extracted text
- Evaluate the representative SCAN PDF already added to the repo against the fallback path
- Extend the parser result shape to record which extraction method was used
- Add a simple heuristic or explicit trigger for when to use the fallback
- Verify that important date-related text that is visible in the PDF becomes available in extracted text

Suggested implementation:
- Parser output fields such as:
- extractionMethod: 'pdf-text' | 'ocr'
- extractionNotes?: string[]
- Fallback trigger based on weak extracted text, missing expected cues, or obviously partial output
- Keep the fallback local-first if possible for the PoC

Why this matters:
The prompt and retrieval layers can only validate what the parser actually extracts. If production-like PDFs do not expose key date fields through the current text extraction path, continuing prompt work would optimize the wrong layer. This ticket reduces that risk early by forcing the PoC to handle a more realistic document shape.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The representative SCAN PDF can be parsed through a fallback-capable path
- The parser can report which extraction method was used
- Date-related text that was missing with the default parser is either recovered or the limitation is explicitly documented
- The fallback behavior is isolated inside the parsing layer rather than leaking into prompt or retrieval code
- The ticket notes clearly state whether the PoC now supports this document type or whether a known limitation remains

Name: AIFA-010 Validation prompt v1
Summary:
Create the first validation prompt focused only on effective dates or contract periods.
- Define the exact input fields the prompt will receive
- Define the retrieved context format the prompt will receive
- Define the exact JSON shape you expect back
- Instruct the model to cite document/page/chunk when making a claim
- Keep the scope intentionally narrow: dates only
- Add guardrails telling the model to say 'not enough evidence' rather than guessing

Suggested output fields:
- field
- outcome
- confidence
- message
- citations

Why this matters:
A narrow, structured prompt is much easier to debug than a broad 'validate everything' prompt. Dates are a good first field family because they are relatively concrete.
Acceptance Criteria:
- There is a written prompt template in code or a dedicated prompt file
- The prompt requests structured JSON output explicitly
- The expected JSON shape aligns with your validation result schema
- The prompt includes citation instructions and anti-guessing language
- A manual test run produces a response that is at least mostly parseable and relevant to date validation only

Name: AIFA-011 Ollama integration
Summary:
Integrate a local LLM via Ollama so the PoC can run without Bedrock.
- Install Ollama locally
- Pull an appropriate model such as llama3 or another supported local model
- Create a small client that sends the prompt and receives the response
- Add transport-level error handling
- Keep the client isolated so it can later be swapped for Bedrock

Suggested functions:
- generateValidation(prompt): Promise<string>

Why this matters:
This lets you prove the full workflow locally without being blocked on AWS access, region enablement, or production provider setup.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A local Ollama model can be called from your code successfully
- Raw model output is captured and logged when needed for debugging
- Common failures such as unavailable Ollama, missing local model, or empty response are handled clearly
- The model client is isolated enough that you can replace it later rather than weaving Ollama calls throughout the codebase

Name: AIFA-011A Normalize and parse validation LLM output
Summary:
Add a small response-normalization and parsing layer for validation results so the pipeline can handle real local LLM output, not just ideal JSON.
- Accept raw model text from the local validation client
- Strip common wrappers such as markdown code fences
- Handle extra leading or trailing explanation text when a JSON payload is still present
- Extract the JSON payload from the raw model response
- Parse the payload into the expected validation result shape
- Fail clearly when no usable JSON can be extracted
- Tighten the prompt wording to discourage markdown fences and extra prose without depending on perfect model compliance

Suggested implementation:
- A small module inside services/ai-form-augmentation/src/validation-output
- One function to normalize raw text
- One function to extract the JSON payload
- One function to parse and validate the result shape
- Prefer simple TypeScript guards first unless a schema library becomes clearly necessary

Why this matters:
Real LLM output often drifts from ideal formatting even when the reasoning is correct. A normalization and parsing layer makes the pipeline resilient enough to work with real local model behavior before UI or storage layers depend on it.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A parser module exists inside services/ai-form-augmentation
- The parser accepts raw LLM output as a string
- The parser can handle common wrapper formats seen in local Ollama responses:
- fenced JSON blocks
- extra leading or trailing explanation text
- The parser returns typed validation results when valid JSON is present
- The parser fails with a clear error when no valid JSON payload can be extracted
- The implementation is verified against at least one real local Ollama response, not only idealized mock JSON
- The prompt is updated to discourage markdown fences and extra prose, but the parser does not depend on perfect model compliance

Name: AIFA-014 Artifact versioning
Summary:
Implement artifact versioning so stale pipeline outputs can be detected and ignored.
- Define how artifactVersion is created, such as hashing the current document keys
- Add artifactVersion to every persisted artifact shape
- Add helper utilities to compute and compare versions
- Document which pipeline steps must read and write versioned artifacts
- Treat this as foundational infra, not a nice-to-have

Why this matters:
Without versioning, a user can change files while older pipeline work is still finishing, and your system may show stale or misleading results. This is a core trust feature.
Acceptance Criteria:
- There is a utility or helper for computing artifactVersion consistently
- artifactVersion is included in the shapes for chunks, status, and validation results
- Version comparison logic exists and is easy to call from resolvers or pipeline code
- A mismatch scenario is documented and testable
- This ticket is completed before any later artifact-writing steps rely on it

Name: AIFA-012 Validation Lambda
Summary:
Create a dedicated Lambda for the validation pipeline.
- Add a Lambda handler entry point for the pipeline
- Deploy it through CDK using patterns consistent with the existing repo
- Give it access to required S3 paths and any other needed permissions
- Keep the GraphQL Lambda as the orchestrator, not the heavy worker
- Make sure logs are easy to find and inspect

Why this matters:
This preserves clean separation between your API layer and the heavier parsing/retrieval/validation work. It also protects the main GraphQL Lambda from memory, timeout, and packaging issues.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The dedicated Lambda deploys successfully through CDK
- It can run at least one end-to-end validation path when triggered
- CloudWatch logs or equivalent are available for debugging
- Permissions are scoped well enough for the PoC to function
- The overall architecture still uses the existing app-api layer as the entry point/orchestrator

Name: AIFA-013 Status tracking
Summary:
Track pipeline progress by writing a versioned status artifact.
- Define a status.json shape with stages such as parsing, embedding, validating, complete, and failed
- Include artifactVersion in the status file
- Write status updates as the pipeline advances
- Include timestamps and useful error text for failed states
- Decide how stale status should be interpreted later by the resolver/UI

Suggested shape:
{
  stage,
  artifactVersion,
  updatedAt,
  error
}

Why this matters:
Status tracking powers the Review page progress UI and gives you observability into long-running work.
Acceptance Criteria:
- status.json is written and updated during pipeline execution
- The file includes artifactVersion, updatedAt, and stage
- Failure states are represented clearly enough for the UI or resolver to react
- Status writes do not silently omit version metadata
- You can manually inspect the file and understand where a pipeline run is currently stuck or completed

Name: AIFA-015 Validation result storage
Summary:
Persist validation results as a versioned artifact.
- Define the JSON shape for validation-result.json
- Include artifactVersion and formSnapshotHash
- Save the result to S3 after validation completes
- Keep the stored result close to the schema expected by the API and UI
- Reconcile model-returned citations against known chunk metadata before trusting fields such as page or order
- Make the file easy to inspect for debugging

Why this matters:
Stored results allow the frontend to poll for completion, enable simple caching, and preserve a clear handoff between pipeline execution and UI display.
Acceptance Criteria:
- validation-result.json is saved successfully
- The file includes artifactVersion and formSnapshotHash
- The stored result shape is consistent with what the UI will eventually consume
- Citation metadata is validated or reconciled against known chunk records rather than trusting echoed LLM values blindly
- The file can be read back and parsed without custom cleanup logic
- This artifact can be used later for cache hits or to avoid re-running validation unnecessarily

Name: AIFA-016 GraphQL trigger resolver
Summary:
Add a GraphQL resolver that triggers the validation Lambda.
- Decide whether this is a mutation or part of an existing flow
- Compute artifactVersion before invoking the worker
- Pass enough identifiers for the Lambda to locate documents and artifacts
- Use async invocation so the request does not block on long-running work
- Add good logging around trigger events

Why this matters:
This is the bridge between the existing app workflow and the new AI worker. It keeps app-api in control while offloading the heavy work to the dedicated Lambda.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The resolver can trigger the validation Lambda asynchronously
- artifactVersion is computed and passed correctly
- The resolver logs enough information to debug invocation failures
- The resolver shape fits existing API patterns in the repo
- A manual test can confirm that a user action in the app could realistically kick off the pipeline

Name: AIFA-017 Status polling resolver
Summary:
Add a GraphQL query/resolver that returns validation status and results for the frontend.
- Read status.json and validation-result.json from S3
- Compare artifactVersion when appropriate
- Return a response shape the Review page can poll repeatedly
- Handle missing artifacts, stale artifacts, failures, and completed results
- Keep the resolver predictable and boring

Why this matters:
This is how the frontend learns whether validation is still running, has failed, or has completed with findings.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The resolver returns the current stage when validation is still running
- The resolver returns stored findings when validation is complete
- Missing or failed artifact cases are handled in a defined way
- Stale artifact/version mismatch scenarios are accounted for
- The response shape is stable enough for the frontend polling loop to depend on

Name: AIFA-018 Review page UI state
Summary:
Add Review page UI states for AI validation progress.
- Decide where the validation status belongs on the Review page
- Start polling the status resolver on page load or the relevant user transition
- Show clear stage messaging such as reviewing documents, validating inputs, and complete
- Make the UI understandable even when validation is unavailable
- Keep the design non-blocking and calm rather than alarming

Why this matters:
The backend may be correct, but users will not trust the feature if the UI is confusing or appears stuck.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The Review page shows a visible AI validation progress state
- The page polls the status resolver successfully
- The UI updates when the backend stage changes
- The messaging is understandable to a non-technical user
- Failure or unavailable states do not break the rest of the page

Name: AIFA-019 Display validation results
Summary:
Render validation findings on the Review page.
- Decide on a UI pattern for warnings and informational findings
- Show message text and citations clearly
- Make it obvious that findings are advisory, not hard blockers
- Ensure the component can render zero findings, one finding, or many findings
- Reuse existing UI patterns where possible instead of inventing a totally new visual language

Why this matters:
This is the user-visible payoff of the whole system. If the results are not understandable, the rest of the pipeline has limited product value.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Findings display correctly on the Review page
- Citations such as document name and page are visible when available
- The UI handles empty, partial, and multiple-result states gracefully
- Results are clearly framed as warnings/advisory output rather than mandatory blockers
- The rendering logic is simple enough to test and maintain

Name: AIFA-019A Wire triggerValidation into Review flow
Summary:
Start AI validation from the real product flow instead of relying on manual or out-of-band triggering.
- Decide the exact trigger point in the Review-page lifecycle
- Call `triggerValidation` at a predictable user-safe moment
- Avoid duplicate triggering on every render, refresh, or poll cycle
- Re-trigger only when the current draft's versioned document set changes
- Keep the experience non-blocking and consistent with the existing status UI

Why this matters:
The backend trigger and polling pieces already exist, but users will only see 'Validation pending' until the product actually starts validation work from a real app flow.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The app calls `triggerValidation` from a defined Review-page or submit-adjacent flow
- Validation does not retrigger on every poll or page refresh without need
- A changed draft document set can cause a fresh validation run when appropriate
- The Review page moves beyond 'Validation pending' after a real in-app trigger
- The trigger behavior is testable from the app, not only from direct API/manual calls

Name: AIFA-019B Local trigger path for ./dev local
Summary:
Make locally triggered validation actually execute when running `./dev local`.
- Choose a local execution strategy for `triggerValidation`
- Either invoke the validation worker directly in local mode or provide a local-compatible shim
- Keep deployed Lambda behavior unchanged
- Ensure local runs still write `status.json` and `validation-result.json`
- Document the local trigger behavior so it does not depend on hidden manual steps

Why this matters:
Without a local trigger path, the frontend can be wired correctly and still never progress beyond 'Validation pending' during local development.
Acceptance Criteria:
- From `./dev local`, a frontend-triggered validation run actually starts
- Local runs write status and result artifacts that the Review page can poll
- The Review page can progress through pending, in-progress, and complete states locally
- Local-only trigger behavior is isolated from deployed Lambda behavior
- Another engineer can reproduce the local trigger flow without rediscovering setup

Name: AIFA-020A Execute full validation pipeline from validationHandler
Summary:
Replace the current status-only validation handler with the real end-to-end pipeline.
- Load the current chunk artifact instead of assuming prompt inputs are already in memory
- Embed stored chunks and build the retrieval index inside the handler
- Build the validation prompt from real retrieved chunks and current form field values
- Call the validation LLM, parse the response, and persist `validation-result.json`
- Update `status.json` across meaningful stages and fail cleanly when a pipeline step breaks
- Keep the local and deployed trigger entry points pointed at the same handler logic

Why this matters:
Right now the Review page can show progress state changes, but the worker does not yet produce real findings from the uploaded documents and form data. Until the handler runs the real pipeline, the PoC is still demonstrating wiring rather than document-backed validation.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A locally or remotely triggered validation run executes the real retrieval/prompt/LLM/result flow
- `validation-result.json` is written by the handler, not only by `src/dev.ts`
- The stored results reflect the current form values and retrieved chunk evidence
- `status.json` advances through real pipeline stages and reports failures with useful error text
- The Review page can display document-backed mismatches after a real product-triggered run

Name: AIFA-020B Display validation citations and evidence details
Summary:
Make Review-page findings useful by showing the evidence behind each validation result.
- Expand the findings UI to show chunk-level citations for each field result
- Display document name, page when available, and any other useful source context already stored in the result artifact
- Distinguish clearly between a high-confidence mismatch, a low-confidence mismatch, and not-enough-evidence
- Keep the rendering readable when a result has multiple citations or no citations
- Preserve the existing non-blocking advisory framing

Why this matters:
An alert banner alone is not enough for a functional PoC. Users need to see what evidence the system found, otherwise they cannot judge whether a mismatch is real, OCR noise, or insufficient evidence.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The Review page shows citations for each validation finding when citations exist
- The UI makes mismatches and not-enough-evidence outcomes understandable without opening dev tools
- Multiple citations render cleanly and consistently
- The evidence display works for both local PoC runs and normal app-triggered runs
- The result presentation is strong enough to demonstrate the end-to-end PoC to another engineer or stakeholder

Name: AIFA-020C Trustworthy start/end validation flow
Summary:
Improve result quality for the two scoped PoC fields: `contractStartDate` and `contractEndDate`.
- Retrieve chunks separately for each field instead of sharing one mixed context
- Split worker status into more meaningful stages such as retrieving, deterministic-validation, and llm-validation
- Add a narrow deterministic validation pass for obvious labeled start/end dates
- Route ambiguous or unresolved cases to the LLM instead of forcing deterministic answers
- Persist `decisionSource` so stored findings can say whether the result came from deterministic logic or the LLM
- Reconcile citations against retrieved chunk metadata before persistence

Why this matters:
The pipeline now runs end to end, but client credibility depends on trustworthy results for start and end dates. This ticket improves the validation core without expanding the field scope or pretending the PoC is production-hardened.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Start and end date retrieval runs independently per field
- Worker status exposes more than one generic validating state
- Obvious labeled start/end dates can be resolved without depending solely on the LLM
- Ambiguous cases defer to the LLM instead of returning forced deterministic answers
- Stored findings include `decisionSource`
- Persisted citations are verified against known retrieved chunks before they reach the UI

Name: AIFA-020D Review-page wording refinement
Summary:
Tighten the Review & Submit wording so the AI results are understandable in a client-facing demo.
- Refine the status copy for in-progress, unavailable, and completed validation states
- Refine findings copy for match, mismatch, and not-enough-evidence outcomes
- Keep the UI advisory and non-blocking
- Avoid backend terminology that a product user would not understand
- Keep the rendering simple enough to test

Why this matters:
The PoC needs to be credible not just in the network tab but in the page the client will actually see. Clear wording is part of trustworthiness, not a cosmetic follow-up.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Review-page status copy is specific and calm
- Findings clearly explain what value was compared against what evidence
- Match, mismatch, and not-enough-evidence outcomes are easy to distinguish
- The wording remains non-blocking and consistent with existing product patterns
- The UI copy changes are covered by focused tests or clear acceptance notes

Name: AIFA-020 Timeout handling
Summary:
Prevent AI validation from trapping the user on the Review page.
- Add a timeout window for polling, such as 90 seconds
- If the timeout is reached, stop treating validation as a blocker for the submit control
- Show a neutral fallback message when validation could not complete
- Make sure failure and unavailable states also allow submission
- Verify that timeout behavior aligns with backend status cases

Why this matters:
An AI enhancement should never make the base product unusable. Timeouts and graceful degradation are part of making this trustworthy.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The submit flow is not permanently blocked by a slow or failed AI run
- After the timeout window, the UI displays a neutral fallback message
- Polling stops or transitions cleanly after timeout
- Failure/unavailable states also allow the user to proceed
- The timeout implementation is consistent with the resolver contract and status stages

Name: AIFA-036 Trigger background validation from Contract Details continue
Summary:
Start AI validation earlier in the form flow by triggering it when the user continues from Contract Details, while keeping Review & Submit as a fallback trigger.
- Add a non-blocking trigger path from the Contract Details continue action when the current draft has both validation date fields and persisted contract documents
- Keep Review & Submit able to trigger validation if the earlier trigger did not run, failed, or the current result later becomes stale
- Reuse the existing triggerValidation mutation and artifact-driven status flow instead of creating a second validation pipeline
- Add a lightweight dedupe guard so continue does not start a redundant run when the current artifactVersion and form snapshot already have a current in-progress or complete validation state
- Preserve the current advisory behavior; navigation from Contract Details must not wait for validation to finish

Why this matters:
Contract Details is the first point in the current workflow where both the relevant dates and the supporting documents are usually present. Triggering validation there lets the work run in the background while the user continues through the form, so Review & Submit is more likely to show ready results instead of starting the process late. The dedupe guard keeps that earlier trigger from turning repeated visits or small workflow loops into unnecessary reruns.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Continuing from Contract Details can start validation in the background when the required validation inputs are present
- The user can continue through the form without waiting for validation to finish
- Review & Submit still works as a fallback trigger when no current validation run/result exists
- Repeated visits to Contract Details do not trigger redundant runs for the same current artifactVersion and form snapshot
- The implementation follows the existing artifactVersion, stale-result, and advisory-status model instead of introducing a parallel trigger state

Name: AIFA-026 Add LaunchDarkly feature flag for validation UI
Summary:
Add a feature flag so the validation experience can be rolled out gradually.
- Decide whether the flag gates just the UI, the backend trigger, or both
- Add flag checks using the repo's existing LaunchDarkly patterns
- Default the feature to off in environments where it is not ready
- Document what behavior happens when the flag is disabled
- Keep the implementation simple and explicit

Why this matters:
Feature flags are useful when rolling out AI features because they let you limit exposure while you learn from real usage and tune accuracy.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A feature flag exists and can disable the validation experience cleanly
- The disabled behavior is defined and tested
- The implementation follows existing LaunchDarkly patterns in the repo
- Turning the flag on exposes the intended experience without additional code changes
- Another engineer can understand exactly what the flag does from the code and ticket notes

Name: AIFA-022 Minimal test corpus
Summary:
Create a small reusable corpus for start/end date validation and the client demo.
- Gather 5 to 10 PDF fixtures
- Record expected outcomes for `contractStartDate` and `contractEndDate`
- Include at least one match case, one mismatch case, and one not-enough-evidence case
- Include at least one ambiguous or multi-date example
- Identify which fixtures are safe and stable enough to use in the demo

Why this matters:
Quality discussions should not be driven by one favorite PDF. A fixed corpus gives the PoC a baseline and gives the demo a repeatable story.
Acceptance Criteria:
- There are 5 to 10 reusable PDF fixtures
- Each fixture has documented expected outcomes for start and end date validation
- The corpus includes match, mismatch, and not-enough-evidence scenarios
- At least one fixture exercises ambiguous or competing date mentions
- A subset of the corpus is marked as the preferred client-demo path

Name: AIFA-023 Validation evaluation harness
Summary:
Measure the current behavior of the PoC against the fixed corpus.
- Run the worker against each corpus fixture
- Compare actual versus expected outcomes
- Track obvious false positives and false negatives
- Record whether the winning result came from deterministic logic or the LLM
- Write down the current limits of local-model quality so future tuning is grounded in evidence

Critical note:
Local models are acceptable for PoC wiring and iteration, but they are not a substitute for production-like quality evaluation. This ticket should make that boundary explicit instead of hiding it.

Why this matters:
The point of the PoC is not just to run. It is to produce results that are credible enough to show and discuss with confidence.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The corpus can be run in a repeatable way
- Each run compares actual outcomes with expected outcomes
- False positives and false negatives are visible instead of anecdotal
- The output notes whether deterministic validation or LLM fallback produced the final result
- The resulting summary is concrete enough to guide the next quality change

Name: AIFA-029 Field-label and retrieval coverage hardening
Summary:
Reduce the intentional narrowness in how the PoC recognizes and retrieves start/end date evidence.
- Expand field config from simple regexes into richer alias sets and retrieval terms
- Allow optional document-family-specific label dictionaries for known contract templates
- Capture enough parsing structure to preserve label proximity or related context, not just raw chunk text
- Improve retrieval inputs so success depends less on exact lexical overlap alone
- Evaluate the changes against the fixed corpus instead of tuning to a single favorite PDF

Why this matters:
Most of the accidental hardcoding has already been removed. The remaining weakness is that the PoC still recognizes evidence too narrowly. This ticket hardens that behavior without expanding scope beyond start and end date validation.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Field config supports richer aliases or retrieval terms for `contractStartDate` and `contractEndDate`
- Known document families can provide label overrides without forking the whole pipeline
- Parsing or chunk metadata preserves enough nearby structure to improve label-to-value matching
- Retrieval behavior is measurably less dependent on exact wording in one document variant
- Corpus results or evaluation notes show whether coverage improved and where limitations still remain

Name: AIFA-030 LLM output robustness and malformed-response handling
Summary:
Harden the model-output boundary so malformed or partial LLM responses do not distort evaluation or runtime behavior.
- Make malformed-output cases visible in the evaluation harness as a separate class of failure
- Tighten the single-field prompt/response contract where it improves reliability without broadening scope
- Keep parser recovery small and explicit if any repair logic is added
- Preserve a safe fallback when the model output cannot be trusted
- Measure how often malformed output occurs across the corpus

Why this matters:
The PoC now has a repeatable corpus and evaluation harness, but malformed LLM output is still a separate reliability risk from retrieval quality. This ticket makes that boundary explicit instead of treating formatting failures like ordinary validation misses.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Evaluation output distinguishes malformed-response failures from normal match/mismatch/not-enough-evidence cases
- The runtime path degrades safely when the LLM output is unusable
- Any prompt or parser changes stay small, explicit, and testable
- Corpus runs can report malformed-output frequency concretely
- Another engineer can tell from the code and notes whether a failure was caused by retrieval quality or output-contract instability

Name: AIFA-031 Reduce malformed LLM output rate
Summary:
Reduce how often the local validation model returns malformed or contract-breaking output without changing the current safe fallback behavior.
- Tighten the date-validation prompt only where it measurably reduces extra prose, wrong shapes, or duplicate field results
- Add one small recovery step only for clearly recoverable responses if prompt hardening alone is not enough
- Compare malformed-output frequency before and after the change using the existing corpus and evaluation harness
- Keep the implementation focused on the single-field validation path rather than broad model experimentation
- Preserve the current fallback and diagnostics behavior introduced by AIFA-030

Why this matters:
AIFA-030 makes malformed responses visible and safe, but it does not reduce how often they happen. This ticket improves the actual reliability of the LLM path so evaluation runs and local demos are less affected by output-contract failures.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Corpus evaluation shows malformed-output frequency before and after the change
- The malformed-output rate is measurably lower on the same corpus or the limitation is documented clearly
- Prompt changes remain narrow and specific to the single-field date-validation contract
- Any recovery logic is small, explicit, and covered by focused tests
- The runtime path still falls back safely and preserves malformed-response diagnostics when output cannot be trusted

Name: AIFA-032 Mismatch message specificity hardening
Summary:
Make mismatch findings specific enough that the Review page reflects the actual document-versus-form difference instead of a coarse year-only summary.
- Tighten the single-field date-validation prompt so mismatch messages include the full submitted date and the full document date when the evidence supports it
- Add a small normalization step after parsing so recoverable year-only mismatch wording can be upgraded to a full-date message from cited evidence when the cited text is parseable
- Add or extend corpus coverage for the AHF term-clause mismatch case so month/day loss is measured instead of treated as anecdotal
- Keep the change narrow to mismatch message wording; do not broaden field scope or rewrite outcome logic
- Preserve the current advisory UI and citation flow

Why this matters:
The current PoC can reach the right mismatch outcome while still understating the actual discrepancy in the stored message. If the UI only says the year is wrong when the month and day are also wrong, the result feels less trustworthy during manual review and demo walkthroughs.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Mismatch findings include the full compared dates when the cited evidence supports full-date comparison
- Year-only mismatch wording is reduced or explicitly documented when the evidence cannot support more precision
- The AHF mismatch case is represented in corpus coverage or equivalent focused evaluation notes
- Any normalization logic stays small, explicit, and limited to recoverable mismatch-message cases
- Existing outcome classification and citation behavior remain unchanged

Name: AIFA-033 Clause-precedence resolution hardening
Summary:
Improve how the worker resolves competing term clauses when one clause appears to supersede weaker header or summary dates.
- Detect precedence cues across competing term clauses instead of treating every multi-date case as the same kind of ambiguity
- Distinguish header or summary dates from stronger operative clause language when the document text supports that distinction
- Recognize amendment-style precedence signals such as amended to read, replaced as follows, shall be, and termination date
- Keep the worker conservative: only resolve to a final date when one clause is clearly stronger; otherwise preserve the conflict-style not-enough-evidence result
- Add corpus coverage for resolvable-precedence cases and genuinely unresolved clause conflicts

Why this matters:
The current PoC now explains conflicting dates better, but it still relies on narrow heuristics when multiple plausible clauses disagree. This ticket improves correctness without backing away from the conservative trust model.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The worker can prefer a clearly superseding operative clause over weaker header or summary date fields when the document text supports that precedence
- Truly conflicting clauses still return not-enough-evidence with a conflict-style message
- Corpus scenarios cover both resolvable-precedence and unresolved-conflict cases
- Existing amendment and AHF competing-date scenarios continue to pass without regression

Name: AIFA-034 Clause-evidence retrieval and fallback hardening
Summary:
Improve clause-evidence recall so precedence logic is more likely to receive the operative amendment or superseding clause text it needs.
- Add clause-oriented retrieval cues for competing-date language such as amended to read, replaced as follows, shall be amended, termination date, and continue in full force and effect
- Improve retrieval inputs so operative clause text is more likely to outrank weaker header or summary date mentions in competing-date fixtures
- Preserve clause-heavy chunk sets for LLM fallback when deterministic precedence cues do not match a known pattern
- Add evaluation coverage for cases where operative clause retrieval succeeds, where clause wording falls outside current deterministic cues, and where retrieval still misses the clause so the result stays conservative
- Keep scope narrow to contractStartDate and contractEndDate; do not broaden field scope or replace the current deterministic layer

Why this matters:
Clause-precedence logic only helps when the worker actually retrieves the operative clause text. This ticket hardens the evidence-retrieval and fallback path so precedence misses can be distinguished from retrieval misses without expanding the PoC beyond its current field scope.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Retrieval inputs or ranking behavior better surface operative amendment or superseding clause text in competing-date scenarios
- Existing precedence logic receives the needed chunk set more reliably on amendment and conflict fixtures
- When deterministic precedence cues still do not match, the LLM fallback receives clause-focused evidence instead of only weaker header or summary dates
- Evaluation output makes clause-retrieval misses visible enough to distinguish retrieval failure from resolver failure
- Existing start and end date scenarios continue to pass without regression

Name: AIFA-035 Deterministic-to-LLM clause-resolution fallback hardening
Summary:
Reduce over-reliance on deterministic regex-style precedence cues when clause-heavy evidence is already retrieved but phrased outside the currently supported patterns.
- Route clause-heavy competing-date evidence into a narrower clause-resolution LLM fallback when deterministic precedence does not find a known cue
- Keep the fallback focused on contractStartDate and contractEndDate only
- Preserve the current conservative behavior when the retrieved clause evidence still supports more than one plausible reading
- Add evaluation coverage for scenarios where clause text is retrieved successfully but deterministic cue matching still misses because the wording differs from the supported patterns
- Keep this as a fallback hardening ticket; do not replace the current deterministic layer or broaden field scope

Why this matters:
Even after clause retrieval improves, the worker can still miss valid operative language when the phrasing does not match the narrow deterministic cue set. This ticket reduces that regex-over-reliance without abandoning the existing trust model or expanding the PoC into general contract interpretation.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Clause-heavy evidence that falls outside the supported deterministic cues can still reach a narrow clause-resolution fallback path
- Unsupported clause phrasing no longer fails only because it misses a regex-style precedence cue
- Ambiguous competing-clause cases still remain conservative and return not-enough-evidence when the fallback cannot justify one final reading
- Evaluation output distinguishes unsupported deterministic phrasing from retrieval misses
- Existing start and end date scenarios continue to pass without regression

Name: AIFA-021 Cache validation results
Summary:
Avoid unnecessary re-validation when the documents and form data have not changed.
- Define the cache key using artifactVersion and formSnapshotHash
- Check for an existing matching validation result before re-running the full validation step
- Return or reuse the cached result when appropriate
- Document when the cache must be invalidated
- Keep the first implementation simple and easy to reason about

Why this matters:
Caching lowers cost, speeds up the user experience, and prevents duplicate model calls when the same review state is revisited.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A cache hit can be detected using artifactVersion and formSnapshotHash
- Matching cached results are reused instead of triggering a new validation call
- Cache invalidation behavior is documented clearly
- The implementation does not accidentally reuse stale results
- The behavior can be demonstrated with a manual or automated test

Name: AIFA-024 Bedrock follow-up for production-like evaluation
Summary:
Prepare the path for evaluating the same validation flow against production-like Bedrock models.
- Reuse the existing provider seams instead of rewriting the pipeline
- Add only the provider work needed to run the evaluation corpus in a production-like environment
- Document packaging, credentials, and account prerequisites honestly
- Do not claim Lambda deployment is solved just because provider seams exist
- Keep this ticket clearly outside the local PoC critical path

Why this matters:
Local Ollama output is good enough for development, but it is not the final quality bar. A later production-like evaluation path should exist without distorting the local-first PoC scope.
Acceptance Criteria:
- There is a defined path for running the corpus against Bedrock-backed providers
- The plan reuses the current EmbeddingProvider and validation-provider seams
- Preconditions for AWS evaluation are documented clearly
- The ticket does not overstate current deployment readiness
- Local PoC progress does not depend on finishing this work first

Name: AIFA-025 Evaluate FAISS implementation behind VectorStore
Summary:
Evaluate whether FAISS is worth introducing after large-submission hardening instead of keeping brute-force retrieval.
- Compare brute-force retrieval against the measured prod-shaped chunk counts and timings
- Separate vector search time from fetch, parse, OCR, chunk, embed, and validation time
- Consider packaging, native dependency friction, Lambda compatibility, and actual scale needs
- Do not assume FAISS is required just because it sounds more advanced
- Keep any spike behind the existing VectorStore abstraction
- Make a keep, defer, or implement recommendation based on measured tradeoffs

Why this matters:
The PoC intentionally avoided premature vector-index complexity. After realistic large-submission measurements and safe work-selection validation, the team can decide whether FAISS solves a real bottleneck.
Acceptance Criteria:
- There is a documented comparison between brute-force retrieval and a FAISS-backed path
- The evaluation uses realistic chunk counts from the prod-shaped fixture
- The evaluation separates vector search time from other pipeline costs
- Operational complexity is considered alongside speed
- Any FAISS work remains behind the VectorStore abstraction
- The team can make an informed keep, defer, or implement decision

Name: AIFA-027 Add cleanup and lifecycle rules for pipeline artifacts
Summary:
Add cleanup behavior for abandoned or deleted draft artifacts.
- Identify which S3 artifact paths need cleanup
- Extend existing cleanup flows if the repo already has a cleanup function
- Add lifecycle expiration rules as a safety net where appropriate
- Document retention assumptions for chunks, status files, and validation results
- Ensure cleanup strategy matches the sensitivity of extracted document text

Why this matters:
AI pipeline artifacts often contain extracted text and intermediate outputs that should not live forever. Cleanup is part of making the system operationally responsible.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Relevant artifact locations are identified and documented
- There is an application-level cleanup path, a lifecycle-rule safety net, or both
- Cleanup behavior is compatible with draft deletion/abandonment flows
- Retention assumptions are written down clearly
- Future production rollout would not leave AI artifacts accumulating indefinitely by default

Name: AIFA-028 Incremental parsing and selective re-indexing
Summary:
Optimize the pipeline so file changes do not always require a full reparse/re-embed.
- Detect added files, removed files, and unchanged files
- Re-parse or re-embed only the changed parts when practical
- Rebuild or update retrieval artifacts accordingly
- Keep the first optimization approach simple and correctness-focused
- Treat this as a post-PoC optimization, not a launch blocker for the initial proof of concept

Why this matters:
The PoC can accept full reprocessing on change, but production systems often benefit from more selective work to reduce latency and cost.
Acceptance Criteria:
- The system can distinguish between changed and unchanged document sets
- At least one incremental path is defined for added or removed files
- The optimization does not compromise correctness or artifact versioning
- The ticket notes clearly explain that the PoC intentionally used full reprocessing as the simpler first implementation
- The resulting behavior is documented well enough that later engineers understand what is optimized and what is not

Name: AIFA-037 Add S3 lifecycle rule safety net for AI validation artifacts
Summary:
Add an S3 lifecycle-rule safety net for the AI validation artifact bucket so old `rag-indexes/` artifacts age out even if scheduled cleanup does not run.
- Scope the rule to the AI validation artifact prefix rather than the whole bucket
- Match or intentionally document the retention window used by the scheduled cleanup path
- Keep application-level cleanup as the primary mechanism; this is a backup retention control
- Document any interaction with versioning if the bucket keeps prior object versions
- Keep the change narrow and infrastructure-focused rather than reshaping the runtime artifact contract

Why this matters:
Scheduled cleanup is useful, but it still depends on application code and operational health. A bucket lifecycle rule gives the PoC a storage-level retention backstop for extracted text and validation artifacts.
Acceptance Criteria:
- A lifecycle rule exists for the AI validation artifact prefix rather than relying only on scheduled cleanup
- The configured retention window is documented and consistent with the intended artifact policy
- The ticket notes make clear that lifecycle is a safety net, not the primary cleanup path
- The change does not broaden retention behavior for unrelated bucket contents
- Any versioned-object limitation or follow-up is documented clearly

Name: AIFA-038 Confirm document metadata and PDF eligibility rules
Summary:
Confirm which document metadata is available and reliable enough to drive PDF eligibility, diagnostics, cache identity, and later work-selection scoring.
- Inspect trigger-time document fields such as name, S3 key, bucket, MIME type, size, ETag, and version ID where available
- Define the minimal PDF eligibility rule for the PoC
- Treat metadata as advisory unless verified
- Decide whether artifactVersion should include only eligible validation documents or all contract documents
- Do not change runtime behavior beyond discovery or small helper validation

Why this matters:
Filtering, cache fingerprinting, and work selection should not be built on assumptions about metadata quality.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Available document metadata is documented
- PDF eligibility rule is explicit and handles missing or inconsistent metadata
- Extension and MIME mismatch behavior is defined
- Renamed non-PDF risk is documented with the intended failure behavior
- artifactVersion impact is decided and documented
- Any helper logic added is covered by focused unit tests

Name: AIFA-039 Add prod-shaped large-submission evaluation fixture
Summary:
Add a synthetic or sanitized evaluation scenario representing a high-document-count submission of roughly 100 to 165 documents.
- Include relevant contract or amendment PDFs
- Include many irrelevant rate/supporting PDFs
- Include unsupported non-PDF inputs or simulated metadata
- Include at least one oddly named relevant document
- Include at least one scanned or weak-text relevant document
- Include a bad or corrupt document case if feasible
- Extend evaluation output with large-run diagnostics

Why this matters:
Large-submission hardening needs a repeatable fixture so performance, retrieval recall, skipped documents, partial coverage, and artifact size are measured instead of guessed.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Evaluation can run a prod-shaped high-document-count scenario without proprietary prod files
- Output reports total documents, eligible documents, skipped documents, failed documents, chunk count, and final outcomes
- Output reports phase timings for fetch, parse, OCR, chunk, embed, retrieval, and validation
- Output reports approximate artifact sizes for parsed text, chunks, vectors, status, and result artifacts where available
- Scenario includes buried or oddly named relevant evidence
- Scenario includes scanned or weak-text relevant evidence

Name: AIFA-040 Filter unsupported validation documents before worker execution
Summary:
Filter unsupported files before invoking the AI worker while preserving diagnostics about ignored documents.
- Apply the PDF eligibility rule from AIFA-038 in app-api
- Pass only eligible validation documents to the worker
- Do not expand support beyond PDFs
- Keep non-PDF behavior explicit and observable
- Do not change the existing upload flow
- Do not treat filename or MIME type as proof that parsing will succeed

Why this matters:
The current worker is PDF-only. Unsupported files should not reach parsePdf and fail the entire validation run.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Unsupported documents are excluded before worker invocation
- Mixed PDF and non-PDF submissions still invoke the worker with eligible PDFs
- All-unsupported submissions fail clearly using existing GraphQL error patterns
- Ignored document count and reason are captured in diagnostics
- Extension and MIME mismatch cases follow the documented eligibility rule
- Files that pass eligibility but fail PDF parsing are handled later by document-level failure isolation, not by this ticket
- Local S3 key derivation still works for eligible PDFs

Name: AIFA-041 Add document-level failure isolation and processing diagnostics
Summary:
Isolate per-document fetch, parse, chunk, and embed failures so one bad document does not fail the whole validation run when other usable PDFs remain.
- Catch per-document processing failures
- Continue indexing successful documents
- Persist skipped, failed, and processed document diagnostics
- Fail only when no eligible documents can be indexed
- Keep user-facing validation conservative when coverage is partial
- Do not silently convert processing failures into not-enough-evidence without diagnostics

Why this matters:
A 165-document submission is likely to contain at least one bad, empty, weak, or malformed file. The worker needs graceful degradation instead of all-or-nothing failure.
Acceptance Criteria:
- One bad document plus valid documents can still complete validation
- All eligible documents failing produces a failed status with useful error text
- Empty-chunk documents are recorded and do not fail the full run when other documents succeed
- Diagnostics distinguish skipped, failed, processed, and usable documents
- Partial processing does not claim all documents were reviewed
- Validation results include or reference coverage diagnostics when not all eligible documents are usable
- Existing normal corpus outcomes do not regress

Name: AIFA-042 Add bounded document indexing concurrency
Summary:
Bound per-document indexing concurrency for fetch, parse, chunk, and embed work.
- Replace unbounded per-document Promise.all behavior with a small concurrency limit
- Keep the limit configurable or clearly centralized
- Preserve document-level failure isolation
- Record enough timing and failure information to distinguish slow but healthy work from stuck work

Why this matters:
Unbounded processing across 100 or more documents can overload CPU, memory, S3, OCR, and embedding runtime.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Document indexing concurrency is bounded
- Default concurrency is conservative for local execution
- Queue continues draining after individual document failures
- Total indexing duration and per-document failure counts are recorded
- Existing small-corpus behavior remains stable
- Focused tests cover concurrency and failure behavior

Name: AIFA-043 Add large-batch OCR safety valve
Summary:
Add a conservative OCR policy for large document batches.
- Preserve existing OCR fallback for small submissions
- Cap or skip OCR fallback in large batches where unlimited OCR would be too expensive
- Record OCR attempted, skipped, and capped counts
- Treat OCR-skipped documents as not fully reviewed
- Keep validation conservative when OCR may contain relevant evidence
- Do not allow OCR skipping to produce a confident match or mismatch by itself

Why this matters:
OCR can dominate first-run cost for large submissions. The PoC needs a safety valve without creating false confidence.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Small submissions keep existing OCR fallback behavior
- Large submissions do not OCR unlimited weak-text PDFs
- OCR-skipped and OCR-capped documents are recorded in diagnostics
- Result wording or diagnostics do not treat OCR-skipped documents as reviewed evidence
- If a field lacks strong evidence and relevant documents were OCR-skipped, the result is partial or conservative rather than confidently complete
- Evaluation includes a case where skipped OCR could contain relevant evidence
- Existing OCR fixture behavior is preserved where applicable

Name: AIFA-044 Increase retrieval breadth with document-diversity guardrails
Summary:
Improve retrieval recall for large document sets without flooding the LLM with unbounded context.
- Retrieve a broader candidate set than the current narrow top-k
- Diversify final evidence by document where appropriate
- Keep final prompt context bounded
- Preserve existing field-specific retrieval behavior
- Report retrieval candidate and final evidence diagnostics
- Do not broaden retrieval in a way that mixes unrelated field evidence into a single prompt

Why this matters:
With 100 or more PDFs, top-3 global chunks can miss controlling start/end date evidence even when the evidence was indexed.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Retrieval considers a broader candidate pool before final context selection
- Final LLM context remains bounded per field
- One noisy date-heavy document cannot dominate all final evidence
- Diagnostics show candidate count, final chunk count, represented documents, and dropped candidate count
- Prod-shaped fixture measures evidence recall
- Broader retrieval does not regress citation reconciliation or field-specific prompting
- Existing clause and date corpus scenarios do not regress

Name: AIFA-049A Add diagnostic-only work-selection scoring
Summary:
Split from the original AIFA-049 work-selection intent. Add explainable document priority scoring and diagnostics without changing which documents are processed.
- Score eligible PDFs using cheap pre-processing signals such as filename, key, metadata, upload order, and form context
- Record score reasons for every eligible document
- Continue processing all eligible PDFs
- Do not hard-exclude low-priority documents
- Use diagnostics to evaluate whether work selection would be safe before changing behavior
- Treat scoring as advisory only and not as evidence

Why this matters:
Diagnostic-only scoring lets us test candidate selection safely before it can affect validation outcomes.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Every eligible document receives a priority score and reason list
- Scoring is explainable and deterministic
- Low-priority documents are still processed
- Diagnostics identify which documents would have been first-pass and deferred
- Prod-shaped fixture reports whether relevant evidence would have been selected early
- Validation outcomes, artifactVersion semantics, and processed document set do not change because of scoring
- No result message or confidence is based on the score

Name: AIFA-045 Evaluate document prioritization and two-pass retrieval
Summary:
Evaluate a conservative two-pass work-selection strategy using diagnostics from AIFA-049A before implementing behavior changes.
- Define first-pass selection rules
- Define fallback triggers
- Define what counts as strong enough first-pass evidence
- Compare metadata, filename, optional text-light scan, and fallback requirements
- Do not implement default work reduction in this ticket
- Require full fallback whenever coverage or evidence quality is uncertain

Why this matters:
Prioritization can improve performance, but it can also miss controlling evidence. The strategy must be evaluated before it changes runtime behavior.
Acceptance Criteria:
- First-pass prioritization cannot suppress fallback unless evidence is clear, field-specific, cited, reconciled, non-partial, and not contradicted by diagnostics
- Oddly named relevant documents are recoverable through fallback rules
- Low-priority documents are deferred, not excluded
- Fallback triggers are documented for not-enough-evidence, ambiguity, missing citations, partial coverage, OCR gaps, failed documents, weak evidence, and conflicting date evidence
- Diagnostics show whether final evidence would come from first pass or fallback
- Produces a concrete recommendation for gated implementation

Name: AIFA-049B Implement gated first-pass selection with conservative fallback
Summary:
Implement the original AIFA-049 work-selection strategy behind an explicit gate or configuration.
- Process prioritized first-pass documents before deferred documents
- Keep low-priority documents deferred, never hard-excluded
- Expand to fallback documents whenever first-pass evidence is not strong enough
- Preserve the existing all-doc behavior as the safe default until promotion
- Record evidence source and fallback reasons
- Keep cache and artifact behavior distinguishable between all-doc and gated runs

Why this matters:
This is the first behavior-changing work-selection step, so it must be reversible, observable, and conservative.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Gated mode can be enabled without changing default behavior
- First-pass processing uses the scoring and strategy defined by AIFA-049A and AIFA-045
- Fallback runs for not-enough-evidence, ambiguity, missing citations, partial coverage, OCR gaps, failed documents, weak field-specific evidence, or conflicting date evidence
- Deferred documents remain eligible for fallback
- Evidence source is recorded as first-pass, fallback, or partial
- Validation artifacts indicate whether the run used all-doc processing or gated work selection
- Existing all-doc processing path remains available

Name: AIFA-048 Surface partial validation coverage conservatively
Summary:
Expose a conservative partial-validation state when some eligible documents were skipped, failed, OCR-capped, deferred beyond max-work, or not fully processed.
- Keep the Review page advisory and non-blocking
- Avoid implying all documents were reviewed when diagnostics show partial coverage
- Summarize partial coverage without overwhelming users
- Preserve existing complete validation UI when coverage is full
- Do not expose internal scoring details as user-facing confidence

Why this matters:
A normal-looking result can mislead users if validation did not process all relevant documents.
Acceptance Criteria:
- API or result layer exposes whether validation coverage is full or partial
- UI copy does not imply all documents were reviewed when diagnostics say otherwise
- Failed, skipped, OCR-capped, deferred, and unprocessed document counts are available to the UI or summarized
- Existing complete validation UI remains unchanged when all eligible documents process successfully
- Submission remains non-blocking
- Partial coverage behavior is covered by focused tests
- User-facing copy remains advisory and does not overstate AI certainty

Name: AIFA-049C Validate and promote work selection after evaluation
Summary:
Complete the original AIFA-049 work-selection intent by validating gated behavior and deciding whether to promote it to default.
- Compare gated first-pass plus fallback behavior against all-doc processing
- Use existing corpus and prod-shaped fixture
- Promote only if outcomes match or become more conservative
- Keep an escape hatch to force full processing
- Document the decision and remaining risks
- Do not promote if partial coverage would become the common path without clear user-facing handling

Why this matters:
Work selection should only become default after evidence shows it does not hide controlling contract evidence.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Existing corpus passes with gated work selection
- Prod-shaped fixture outcomes match all-doc processing or become more conservative
- Evaluation reports when evidence came from first pass versus fallback
- Evaluation reports how often fallback was required and why
- Default enablement decision is documented
- Escape hatch for full processing remains available
- Remaining known failure modes are documented

Name: AIFA-046 Split parsed-text artifacts from embedding artifacts
Summary:
Persist per-document parsed text separately from chunk and embedding artifacts.
- Store extracted text, page text, extraction method, and extraction notes as reusable per-document artifacts
- Rebuild chunks and embeddings from parsed text when appropriate
- Avoid rerunning expensive parse or OCR work when only embedding or chunk artifacts need regeneration
- Keep artifact paths under existing validation artifact lifecycle assumptions
- Do not reuse parse artifacts when source document identity is uncertain

Why this matters:
Parsing and OCR are expensive and have different invalidation behavior than embeddings. Splitting artifacts reduces repeated work without changing validation scope.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Parsed-text artifact can be reused independently from embeddings
- Extraction method and notes are preserved
- Failed or partial parse artifacts are not reused as successful parse artifacts
- Cleanup and lifecycle assumptions are updated for the new artifact path
- Existing document-index reuse remains correct
- Artifact size impact is measured or reported
- Reuse requires the same document identity inputs used by the current cache logic until AIFA-047 strengthens identity

Name: AIFA-047 Strengthen document cache identity with content fingerprints
Summary:
Strengthen document cache identity so unchanged S3 keys do not accidentally reuse stale parse, chunk, or embedding artifacts after content changes.
- Prefer cheap reliable metadata such as version ID where available
- Document ETag and multipart upload limitations
- Measure overhead of the chosen fingerprint approach
- Use full content hashing only if cheaper metadata cannot safely identify changes
- Preserve artifactVersion and per-document cache consistency
- Do not silently reuse artifacts when fingerprint confidence is insufficient

Why this matters:
Current document reuse keys off document name plus S3 bucket/key identity. If content is overwritten in place, stale artifacts could be reused.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Cache identity changes when document content changes at the same key
- Chosen fingerprint source is documented with limitations
- Missing fingerprint metadata falls back safely and observably
- Existing cache reuse still works for unchanged documents
- artifactVersion and per-document cache behavior remain aligned
- Fingerprint overhead is measured or documented
- Low-confidence fingerprint cases are visible in diagnostics

Name: AIFA-050 Apply work-selection promotion decision to runtime default
Summary:
Apply the evaluated work-selection promotion decision to runtime configuration without weakening the current conservative safeguards.
- Make runtime work-selection default explicit instead of implicitly remaining `all-doc`
- Allow promoted `gated-first-pass` default only when the evaluated decision supports it
- Preserve an explicit `all-doc` override for debugging and safety
- Keep conservative fallback behavior from AIFA-049B unchanged
- Keep partial-coverage handling from AIFA-048 accurate under the runtime default

Why this matters:
The evaluation harness now records when gated work selection is safe enough to promote, but the product runtime still pays the full `all-doc` cost on large submissions unless that decision is applied deliberately.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Runtime default work-selection mode is explicit
- Promoted `gated-first-pass` default is only enabled when the evaluated decision supports it
- An explicit `all-doc` override remains available
- Conservative fallback behavior is unchanged
- Partial-coverage messaging remains accurate under the selected runtime default
- Focused tests cover default selection and override behavior

Name: AIFA-051 Persist indexing-phase progress during long validation runs
Summary:
Persist bounded indexing progress during long-running validation so status artifacts show forward movement before retrieval begins.
- Update `status.json` during document indexing instead of only at phase boundaries
- Persist enough progress information to distinguish active work from a stalled run
- Keep stage semantics compatible with existing UI polling and stale/current artifact handling
- Keep the change diagnostic-only and avoid changing validation outputs or fallback behavior
- Avoid writing progress so frequently that artifact churn becomes noisy

Why this matters:
Large local submissions can spend many minutes indexing documents while `status.json` stays frozen at `parsing`, which makes active progress hard to distinguish from a wedged worker.
Acceptance Criteria:
- `status.json` updates during long indexing runs
- Persisted progress includes enough information to show forward movement conservatively
- Existing Review-page polling remains compatible with the updated status artifact shape
- Validation results, fallback behavior, and work selection remain unchanged
- Focused tests cover indexing-progress writes and terminal status behavior

Name: AIFA-052 Add LLM-assisted first-pass reranking for large low-yield documents
Summary:
Add an optional LLM-assisted reranking step ahead of first-pass indexing so very large low-yield documents can be deprioritized more intelligently without being excluded from conservative fallback.
